source: Invest Like the Best — The cyclone under the surface (EP.260)
summary: Persistent wage inflation would be severely negative for all equities, but tech would be relatively better positioned
summary_extended: Baker argues that if wage inflation proves persistent, it would be severely damaging to all equities through the mechanism Buffett identified in the 1970s: inflation inflates the asset base required to run a business, which in turn depresses ROE and ROIC and compresses equity valuations. However, Baker contends that modern large-cap tech companies are structurally better positioned than the rest of the market to withstand this environment because they are asset-light, generate the highest ROICs of any sector, possess massive pricing power, and employ the fewest workers per dollar of gross profit — the precise inverse of the capital-intensive, low-ROIC businesses that were crushed in the 1970s inflationary episode. The investment implication is a relative value view: tech should outperform the broader equity market in a sustained wage inflation scenario, even if tech itself declines in absolute terms. Baker frames this as a conditional and humble macro view, explicitly acknowledging uncertainty about whether wage inflation is truly structural, and does not name specific companies as beneficiaries.
detail: Using Buffett's 1970s framework, Baker explains that inflation inflates the asset base and depresses ROE/ROIC, compressing equity returns. However, modern tech companies are asset-light with highest ROICs, massive pricing power, and lowest employees per dollar of gross profit — the inverse of 1970s tech. So while all equities suffer from wage inflation, tech should outperform on a relative basis.
confidence_evidence: Baker frames this as a conditional view ('if wage inflation is here to stay') and explicitly caveats with humility about macro forecasting.
quote_fragment: tech companies today, they're super asset-like...highest ROICs...massive pricing power
